# Transcript: 05\_NLP\_Basic\_4

**Duration** : N/A **Language** : en

## Transcript Content

**[00:00:00]** Welcome back.

**[00:00:10]** This module we are going to talk about basic natural language processing and how it relates

**[00:00:15]** to the text mining in Python that we have been talking about.

**[00:00:20]** So what is natural language?

**[00:00:22]** Well any language that is used in everyday communication by humans is natural language.

**[00:00:28]** Let's compare to something that is artificial language or a computer language like Python.

**[00:00:35]** Languages such as English or Chinese or Hindi or Russian or Spanish are all natural languages

**[00:00:40]** but you know also the language we use in short text messages or on tweets is also by this

**[00:00:48]** definition natural language isn't it?

**[00:00:51]** So then we have to kind of address these as well.

**[00:00:54]** So then what is natural language processing?

**[00:00:48]** definition natural language isn't it?

**[00:00:51]** So then we have to kind of address these as well.

**[00:00:54]** So then what is natural language processing?

**[00:00:57]** Any computation or manipulation of natural language to get some insights about how words

**[00:01:03]** mean and how sentences are constructed is natural language processing.

**[00:01:10]** One thing to consider when we look at natural language is that these evolve.

**[00:01:15]** For example new words get added like selfie or photobomb.

**[00:01:21]** Old words lose popularity.

**[00:01:24]** How often have you used thou shalt?

**[00:01:27]** Meaning of words change.

**[00:01:29]** Words such as learn in old English meant exactly opposite of what it means now.

**[00:01:34]** It used to mean teach.

**[00:01:37]** And then language rules themselves may change.

**[00:01:39]** So for example in old English the position of the verb was at the end of the sentence rather

**[00:01:37]** And then language rules themselves may change.

**[00:01:39]** So for example in old English the position of the verb was at the end of the sentence rather

**[00:01:44]** than the middle as we come to know today.

**[00:01:48]** So when we talk about NLP tasks what do we mean?

**[00:01:53]** It could mean as simple as counting the words or counting the frequency of a word or

**[00:01:59]** finding unique words in a corpus.

**[00:02:01]** And then build on to find sentence boundaries or parts of speech.

**[00:02:07]** So tag a sentence with its parts of speech.

**[00:02:12]** Pass the sentence structure.

**[00:02:13]** Try to understand more grammatical constructs and see whether they apply for a particular sentence.

**[00:02:20]** Identify semantic roles of how these words play.

**[00:02:24]** For example if you have a sentence like Mary loves John then you know that Mary is the subject.

**[00:02:20]** Identify semantic roles of how these words play.

**[00:02:24]** For example if you have a sentence like Mary loves John then you know that Mary is the subject.

**[00:02:33]** John is the object and love is the verb that connects them.

**[00:02:39]** You have the other NLP tasks like identifying entities in a sentence.

**[00:02:45]** So this is called named entity recognition.

**[00:02:47]** And in our previous example of Mary loves John, Mary and John are the two entities, both persons in that sentence.

**[00:02:58]** And then you could have more complicated, more complex tasks like finding which pronoun refers to which entity.

**[00:03:08]** This is called co-reference resolution or co-reference resolution.

**[00:03:12]** And there are many, many more tasks that you would do on free text.

**[00:03:19]** The challenge is how to do that in an efficient manner and how it applies to overall text mining.

**[00:03:12]** And there are many, many more tasks that you would do on free text.

**[00:03:19]** The challenge is how to do that in an efficient manner and how it applies to overall text mining.

**[00:03:26]** And you're going to see some of those in the next few videos.
